# Experiment: Four Distinct Visions

**Generated by:** Opus subagent
**Date:** 2026-03-24

## VISION-A: Intent Authority (7/10 gravity)

**Problem:** Intent is scattered across specs, ADRs, frontmatter, AGENTS.md, and the operator's head. No unified, queryable graph of "what has been decided."

**Value prop:** Makes "what has been decided?" a question with a computable answer. Elements indexed into a queryable graph with governance edges (what governs what, what supersedes what).

**Assessment:** Medium-High gravity. The graph-of-decisions concept is rich enough to drive multiple initiatives. But constantly wants to reference Evidence and Reconciliation — feels slightly academic without them. Partially overlaps VISION-001's existing scope.

## VISION-B: Execution Fidelity (4/10 gravity)

**Problem:** Execution is a black box. Swain captures intent and verifies outcomes, but the process of execution — how the agent decomposed the spec, what constraints it consulted, what it skipped — is invisible.

**Value prop:** Makes the transition from intent to implementation observable and governable.

**Assessment:** Weakest of the four. Collapses into VISION-005 (Trustworthy Agent Governance). Strip away Intent and Evidence references and you're left with "agents should follow rules" — already covered. The execution record concept adds something new, but it's more naturally an initiative under a broader vision.

## VISION-C: Observable Reality (9/10 gravity)

**Problem:** Swain has no systematic model of what is actually true. Evidence is scattered and ephemeral — git history is unstructured, test results are consumed and discarded, agent sessions aren't indexed. Answering "what is actually happening?" requires ad hoc investigation every time.

**Value prop:** Gives swain a structured, persistent, queryable model of what is actually true — derived from verifiable sources, not declarations.

**Assessment:** The surprise winner. Most independent substance. Solves real, standalone problems even without the loop (session context loading, "what changed while I was away?"). Most underserved area in swain. Least referential dependency — "the system should know what is true" stands on its own.

## VISION-D: Structural Reconciliation (6/10 gravity)

**Problem:** Intent drifts from reality silently. No systematic model of what reconciliation is, what it should cover, when it should run, or what it should produce.

**Value prop:** Systematically compares intent graph against evidence layer. Produces structured reports: what matches, what drifts, what's missing, what actions are recommended.

**Assessment:** Conceptual weight but fundamental dependency problem — defined as the comparison of Intent and Evidence. Without those two layers being structured, reconciliation reduces to "run some scripts and see what looks different." More of a consequence of getting A and C right.

## Summary

| Vision | Gravity | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Intent Authority | 7/10 | Real but overlaps VISION-001 |
| Execution Fidelity | 4/10 | Collapses into VISION-005 |
| Observable Reality | 9/10 | Clear standalone winner |
| Structural Reconciliation | 6/10 | Important but dependency-heavy |

**The four-vision structure is lopsided.** The subagent suggested an alternate: two new visions (Observable Reality + Reconciliation), evolve VISION-001 to include intent-graph, evolve VISION-005 to absorb execution concerns.
